text:
Steven van de Velde is one of the most controversial athletes competing at the2024 OlympicsinParis.

It’s rare to see a criminal convicted of such a shocking crime taking part in any major sporting event.

But the 29-year-old fromthe Netherlandswill be in the spotlight when he takes to the sand for his first beach volleyball match in theFrench capitalon Sunday morning.

His inclusion in the Dutch team has caused controversy and his own national committee has taken extra measures tokeep him away from the Olympic village.

He will also not take part in any media duties after his matches, which will instead be left to his teammate Matthew Immers.

Here is a look at who Steven van de Velde is,the crime he committedand everything we know about him.

Steven van de Velde isa Dutch volleyball playerwho was born in The Hague in the Netherlands in August 1994.

He’s married to a fellow volleyball pro from Germany, Kim Behrens, and they have one child together.

His brother-in-law Kevin Behrens is also a sports star who plays for Bundesliga team VfL Wolfsburg and the Germany national football team.

Van de Velde is known for his imposing height, standing at 6ft 6in tall.

Van de Veldepleaded guilty to three counts of child rapeagainst a 12-year-oldgirl fromMilton Keynesat Aylesbury Crown Court in 2016.

He had met the schoolgirl on Facebook and flew to the UK in August 2014 before travelling to her home in Buckinghamshire, where he sexually abused her. He also raped her at the nearby Furzton Lake.

The court heard how he only stopped when she complained that she was in pain.

Following the attacks, the child suffered emotionally, which led to her self-harming and even overdosing on drugs on one occasion.

The sportsman was aged 19 at the time of the offences and knew how old his victim was – but had told her he was two years younger than he was.

Yes.Van de Velde was sentenced to four years in prisonafter he was extradited to the UK and arrested in January 2016. He was jailed after his guilty plea in March 2016.

Judge Francis Sheridan told Van de Velde in court: ‘Your hopes of representing your country now lie as a shattered dream.’

His own defence lawyer, Linda Strudwick, said: ‘He’s lost a stellar sporting career and he’s being branded a rapist. It’s plainly a career end for him.’

He was placed on the sex offenders register for life and was told he would be deported back to Holland at the end of his sentence.

Van de Velde was transferred from the UK back to the Netherlands to serve his sentence under a treaty between the two countries.

It then allowed for his charges and sentence to be adjusted in line with Dutch law, meaning the charge of rape was changed to ‘fornication’.

He was released from prison in 2017, having only served one year of his original sentence, and Van de Velde said a year later: ‘I cannot reverse it, so I will have to bear the consequences. It has been the biggest mistake of my life.’

The NSPCC condemned the decision for Van de Velde to be released three years early and said his ‘lack of remorse and self-pity is breathtaking’.

It added: ‘We can only begin to imagine how distressed his victim must feel if she sees his comments.’

The Dutch Volleyball Association allowed him to continue his career as a beach volleyball player.

The Dutch Olympic Committee (NOC) is responsible for selecting its athletes for the Olympics, and theInternational Olympic Committee (IOC), which governs the games, said it would not intervene.

After picking him, the NOC said: ‘After his release, Van de Velde sought and received professional counselling. He demonstrated to those around him – privately and professionally – self-insight and reflection.’

Last month, Dutch volleyball association Nevobo also gave Van de Velde their backing and said there was ‘no reason’ to exclude him.

Michel Everaert, general director at Nevobo, said: ‘He is proving to be an exemplary professional and human being and there has been no reason to doubt him since his return. We fully support him and his participation in Paris, which he and Matthew have earned.’

Steven van de Velde’s first beach volleyball match is on Sunday, July 28 at 9am UK time against Italy.

He will be playing alongside Matthew Immers, 23, and the pair are in Pool B.

They will play at least three games, with their second match on Wednesday, July 31 at 3pm versus Chile and their third on Friday, August 2 at 7pm against Norway.

If they qualify from the pools, Van de Velde could play as many as seven matches in total en-route to a potential gold medal final or bronze medal play-off.

Van de Velde has enjoyed a successful volleyball career, including winning the Under-20 Dutch National Championships in 2011 when he was 17.

He went on to represent the Netherlands at the 2015 European Games – which fell between the time he committed his crimes and when he was actually extradited to the UK.

Such was his success; Van de Velde was tipped to become an Olympian at the 2016 Olympics before his conviction and prison sentence.

Since his release, he notably finished third at the 2018 FIVB Beach Volleyball World Tour and won silver at an event in Brazil in May this year, before being selected for the Netherlands national team at the 2024 Olympics.

title: Who is Steven van de Velde? The convicted rapist at the Paris Olympics | Metro News
description: Steven van de Velde, beach volleyball player from the Netherlands, is one of the most controversial competitors at the 2024 Olympic Games.
